steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a text structure and purpose question. The passage, adapted from Jerome K. Jerome’s 1889 novel, opens with the claim that people are “creatures of the sun” who love light and life — and that this is why they crowd into towns and cities. The rest of the passage develops that psychological drive, contrasting daytime comfort in nature with nighttime loneliness that sends people back toward gas-lit streets and human voices. The decisive clue is the explicit causal statement: “That is why we crowd into the towns and cities.”

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — To address common traits that motivate many people to choose to live in urban environments: Directly matches the passage’s causal argument: love of light, life, and human connection are the psychological traits driving people to cities. ✓
  • Choice B — To indicate that people sometimes experience feelings of isolation in crowded areas: The passage describes loneliness in the dark countryside — not in crowded cities; this reverses the passage’s logic. ✗
  • Choice C — To illustrate the idea that most people tend to prefer hillsides in the country to certain aspects of towns and cities: The passage says the opposite — people prefer cities, especially at night; country hillsides are only pleasant during the day. ✗
  • Choice D — To caution people not to be so quick to dismiss the natural beauty that can be found in rural areas: The passage makes no such cautionary point; it explains why people move to cities, not why they should value the countryside. ✗
A is correct.
